title: Your First WebAssembly Program and Web App (Written, Tested, and Deployed Entirely in the Web Browser)
optimized_headline: Build and Deploy Your First WebAssembly App Directly in Your Browser
raw_excerpt:
Compiling and running C code with Emscripten and GitHub Codespaces — no local installation required.
The post Your First WebAssembly Program and Web App (Written, Tested, and Deployed Entirely in the Web Browser) appeared first on Towards Data Science.
raw_body:
Compiling and running C code with Emscripten and GitHub Codespaces — no local installation required.
The post Your First WebAssembly Program and Web App (Written, Tested, and Deployed Entirely in the Web Browser) appeared first on Towards Data Science.
speedrun: A new guide shows how to compile and run C code directly in the browser using WebAssembly, Emscripten, and GitHub Codespaces. This method eliminates the need for local installations, making it easier for developers to test and deploy applications. With this approach, users can write, test, and deploy code entirely online. This shift could streamline development processes and encourage more experimentation in web programming.
why_it_matters: Developers can quickly prototype applications without the hassle of local setup, fostering innovation and speed.
This trend signals a broader move towards cloud-based development environments, which could reshape how software is built and deployed.

lenses.eli12: Imagine being able to bake a cake without needing an oven at home. This guide lets you write and run C code right in your web browser, thanks to WebAssembly. It simplifies the coding process, making it accessible for more people. This could encourage more folks to try programming without technical barriers.
lenses.pm: For product managers and founders, this means faster prototyping and testing of ideas without the usual setup costs. Streamlined development could lead to quicker iterations and reduced time to market. This method also opens opportunities for remote collaboration among teams.
lenses.engineer: From a technical standpoint, using Emscripten with GitHub Codespaces allows developers to compile C code into WebAssembly directly in the browser. This avoids local dependencies and speeds up the testing process. It highlights the growing capabilities of web technologies, though developers should remain aware of performance trade-offs when using WebAssembly.
